answer: DynamoDB costs depend on data storage, read, and write requests; for a 10TB dataset, expect significant monthly charges, especially with high throughput. Use DynamoDB's pricing calculator for precise estimates.

results.title: Understanding DynamoDB Cost Spikes Through Real Usage ...
results.snippet: Once you’ve understood the basics, like the nutty **7.5x inflation of on-demand compared to reserved**, or the excessive costs around item size, replication and caching … you’ll realize that DynamoDB costs aren’t just about read/write volume – it’s a lot more nuanced in the real-world. Because DynamoDB meters out the costs in rounded-up 1KB chunks for writes (and 4KB for reads). All that data transfer and (replicated) write/delete activity adds up fast … even though you’re trying to “clean up.” We discussed these tricky situations in detail in a recent DynamoDB costs webinar, which you can now watch on-demand. Our DynamoDB cost calculator lets you simulate cache hit ratios, data set sizes, instance types and nodes. And finally, if you’re a team with unpredictable costs and performance using DynamoDB, make the switch to ScyllaDB and enjoy the benefits of predictable pricing, built-in efficiency and more control over your database architecture.

results.title: Amazon DynamoDB Pricing: A 2025 Cost Savings Guide - CloudZero
results.snippet: | Write Request | API calls to write data to your tables   Types: Standard and  Transactional | Write Request Units (WRUs) for On-Demand Mode   Write Capacity Units (WCUs) for Provisioned Mode |. DynamoDB On-Demand capacity mode prices depend on the type of request (read, write, or storage), operation (transactional, eventually consistent, strongly consistent), table class you use (Standard, Standard Infrequent Access), and AWS Region you are based in. **Pricing for data storage in DynamoDB’s On-Demand capacity mode starts at $0.25 per GB-month** for the Standard table class and $0.10 for the Standard Infrequent Access tale class**.**. | DynamoDB Streams | GetRecords API call (streams read request unit) returns up to 1MB of data from Streams | $0.02 per 100,000 streams read request units after the first 2.5 million (which are free every month) |. | DynamoDB Streams | GetRecords API call (streams read request unit) returns up to 1MB of data from Streams | $0.02 per 100,000 streams read request units after the first 2.5 million (which are free every month) |.

results.title: Amazon DynamoDB Pricing: Why GSIs Triple Your Bill (2026)
results.snippet: Amazon DynamoDB pricing looks simple at first glance: pick a capacity mode, pay for reads, writes, and storage. Your total cost depends on four dimensions: throughput (reads and writes), storage, backups, and add-on features like Global Tables or DAX. The second layer is **add-on features** like storage, backups, DynamoDB Streams, Global Tables, and DAX, which each add incremental costs on top of throughput. A 500 GB table with minimal read/write traffic costs $125/month on Standard vs $50/month on Standard-IA. A Global Table with 3 replicas generating 42.5 million writes per month now costs roughly $8.71/month, down from $26.56 before the price cut. The capacity mode choice has the largest cost impact, and after the November 2024 price cut, on-demand is the right starting point for most workloads. Run your workload through the DynamoDB pricing calculator to compare costs across on-demand, provisioned, and commitment-discounted options before you commit. Database Savings Plans offer up to 18% discount on DynamoDB on-demand throughput and up to 12% on provisioned capacity.

results.title: How DynamoDB Pricing Works | Yugabyte
results.snippet: In a nutshell, DynamoDB’s monthly cost is dictated by data storage, writes and reads. This feature of DynamoDB allows you to deploy your tables across multiple AWS regions. Write capacity for global tables is represented by replicated WCUs instead of standard WCUs. Don’t forget about the fact that you are now transferring data between AWS Regions, so expect additional fees. AWS offers DynamoDB Streams, which is a time-ordered sequence of item-level changes on a DynamoDB table. DynamoDB performs two underlying reads or writes of every item in the transaction, one to prepare the transaction and one to commit the transaction. DynamoDB charges you for the reads and writes that your application performs on your tables in terms of read request units and write request units. If an application grows very quickly with increasingly larger data volumes that need to be stored and served, and if low latency and geo-distribution are must-have requirements for driving customer satisfaction, then DynamoDB becomes a cost-prohibitive choice that will also slow down release velocity.

results.title: AWS DynamoDB Pricing - Cost Breakdown & Savings Guide
results.snippet: # AWS DynamoDB Pricing - Cost Breakdown & Savings Guide. **Amazon DynamoDB** is one of the most preferred tools in the modern age. However, let’s face it, for early-stage companies, developers, and anyone interested in the cloud, understanding the **pricing of the Amazon DynamoDB** can be very challenging. it’s local to the use of the system and the rate plan selected. Miscalculating these pricing models could lead to high costs placed on your AWS account in the long run. **Don’t sweat it**—in this blog, we will explain **DynamoDB pricing** better, how to cut costs when optimizing DynamoDB for an application, and how to spend smarter while remaining within my budget. ## **What is Amazon DynamoDB?**. **Amazon DynamoDB** tries to be the middleman between the resources provided and the end product being used. In this case, it is a fully functioning NoSQL database that can function at a high volume, allowing it to handle many requests at a singular time.
